pattern: Since a peak or valley only ever depends on three consecutive digits, a digit-by-digit count-and-sum walk that remembers the previous two digits (and whether it is still bounded by the upper limit) can total the waviness of every number up to N without ever visiting them one by one.
hints: Split the problem by digit length: every length shorter than N's own length is completely unrestricted, and only the numbers with exactly N's own digit count need to respect the 'at most N' limit.

For a fixed length, build the count digit by digit while remembering the previous two digits placed; each time you place a new digit, you can immediately tell whether the digit before it turned out to be a peak or a valley.

Track two running totals together at every state -- how many completions are possible, and how much total waviness they carry -- so that placing the next digit lets you add that digit's contribution to every one of those completions at once, without enumerating them.

edge_inputs.description: a single number with no peaks or valleys, too short
a single three-digit number that is itself a peak
a single three-digit number that is itself a valley
smallest possible range, num1 equals num2 at the minimum bound
a wide range that pushes into six-digit numbers
a number with multiple interior peaks and valleys back to back
a range crossing a round-number boundary where digit lengths change
monotonic digits produce zero waviness despite being multi-digit

stress.description: random sub-ranges across the full fifteen-digit constraint span
stress.generator_python:
def gen(rng, size):
    hi = max(1, min(size, 1000000000000000))
    a = rng.randint(1, hi)
    b = rng.randint(a, hi)
    return [a, b]
